title: Gastroenterology — GI & Hepatology USMLE
description: Peptic ulcer disease, IBD, liver disease, pancreatitis, colon cancer screening, GI bleeding. High-yield USMLE & clinical medicine.
subject: medicine

cards.front: H. pylori: most common test for active infection
cards.back: Urea breath test or stool antigen test. Biopsy (CLO test) if EGD performed. Serology only shows exposure, not active infection
PPI + clarithromycin + amoxicillin × 14 days (or metronidazole if penicillin allergy)

cards.front: Most common cause of peptic ulcer disease
cards.back: H. pylori (~60–70%). Second: NSAID use (~20–30%)

cards.front: Duodenal vs gastric ulcer: pain timing
cards.back: Duodenal: pain relieved by eating (food buffers acid). Gastric: pain worsened by eating
Gastrinoma (usually in duodenum or pancreas) → excessive gastric acid → multiple recurrent ulcers. ↑ gastrin, ↑ basal acid output. Part of MEN-1

cards.front: Crohn's disease vs ulcerative colitis: location
cards.back: Crohn: any part GI tract (mouth to anus), skip lesions, transmural. UC: rectum extending proximally, continuous, mucosa/submucosa only
Fistulas, strictures, abscesses, terminal ileum involvement → B12/fat malabsorption, calcium oxalate kidney stones (fat binds Ca → free oxalate absorbed)
Risk increases with extent of disease and duration. Surveillance colonoscopy starting 8 years after pancolitis diagnosis, every 1–2 years
Joints: ankylosing spondylitis, peripheral arthritis. Skin: pyoderma gangrenosum, erythema nodosum. Eyes: uveitis, episcleritis. Liver: primary sclerosing cholangitis (UC > Crohn)
Strongly associated with UC (70–80% of PSC patients have IBD). Beaded appearance of bile ducts on ERCP/MRCP. ↑ risk of cholangiocarcinoma
Anti-tissue transglutaminase (tTG) IgA (most sensitive/specific). Histology: villous atrophy, crypt hyperplasia, intraepithelial lymphocytes
Strict lifelong gluten-free diet. Monitor with tTG antibody titers

cards.front: Hepatitis B serology: HBsAg, anti-HBs, HBeAg meaning
cards.back: HBsAg: surface antigen (active infection). Anti-HBs: protective antibody (resolved infection or vaccination). HBeAg: high viral replication/infectivity
HBsAg negative, anti-HBs negative, anti-HBc IgM positive — the only positive marker during window period

cards.front: Hepatitis C: most common route of transmission in US
cards.back: Injection drug use (IVDU). Sexual transmission less efficient than HBV. No vaccine available
Fecal-oral transmission. High mortality in pregnant women (20%). Found in developing countries; no chronic form
Fatty liver (reversible) → alcoholic hepatitis (AST:ALT > 2:1) → cirrhosis (irreversible)
Portal hypertension → varices, ascites, splenomegaly. Hepatic encephalopathy. Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis. Hepatorenal syndrome. Hepatocellular carcinoma
Bilirubin, albumin, PT/INR, ascites, encephalopathy (BAEP + A = 5 items). Scores A/B/C; Child C = poor surgical candidate
Ascitic fluid PMN count > 250 cells/mm³. Most common organism: E. coli, Klebsiella, Streptococcus pneumoniae. Treatment: cefotaxime IV
Type 1: rapid doubling of creatinine in < 2 weeks (poor prognosis). Type 2: slower progression, refractory ascites. No structural kidney disease
Autosomal recessive ATP7B gene. Copper accumulation → hepatitis, cirrhosis, neuropsychiatric symptoms, Kayser-Fleischer rings (corneal copper deposits). Treatment: penicillamine or trientine
Autosomal recessive HFE gene (C282Y mutation). Iron overload → bronzed skin, cirrhosis, diabetes, cardiomyopathy, hypogonadism. Treatment: phlebotomy
Gallstones (40–70%) and Alcohol (30%). Mnemonic: GET SMASHED (Gallstones, Ethanol, Trauma, Steroids, Mumps, Autoimmune, Scorpion sting, Hypercalcemia/Hypertriglyceridemia, ERCP, Drugs)
Age > 55, WBC > 16k, Glucose > 200, LDH > 350, AST > 250 (GA LAS)

cards.front: Cullen's sign vs Grey Turner's sign (pancreatitis)
cards.back: Cullen: periumbilical ecchymosis. Grey Turner: flank ecchymosis. Both indicate retroperitoneal hemorrhage; poor prognosis
NPO (rest pancreas), aggressive IV fluid resuscitation (lactated Ringer preferred), analgesia, ERCP if gallstone pancreatitis with cholangitis
Epigastric pain (radiating to back), steatorrhea (fat malabsorption due to lipase deficiency), diabetes (islet destruction)

cards.front: Pancreatic cancer: most common type and risk factors
cards.back: Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma. Risk factors: smoking, chronic pancreatitis, diabetes, BRCA2, FAP, Lynch syndrome
Painless jaundice + non-tender palpable gallbladder → suggests pancreatic/biliary cancer (not gallstones, which cause scarring/fibrosis of GB)

cards.front: Upper GI bleed: most common cause
cards.back: Peptic ulcer disease. Others: Mallory-Weiss tear (forceful vomiting), esophageal varices, Dieulafoy lesion

cards.front: Lower GI bleed: most common cause by age
cards.back: < 40: IBD, Meckel's diverticulum. > 40: diverticulosis (most common), angiodysplasia, colorectal cancer, ischemic colitis
Colonoscopy every 10 years starting at age 45 (ACS) or 50 (USPSTF). FIT/gFOBT annually. Earlier if family history, IBD, FAP, Lynch
Autosomal dominant mismatch repair gene defects (MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2). Most common hereditary CRC syndrome. Also ↑ risk endometrial, ovarian, urinary cancer

cards.front: Familial adenomatous polyposis (FAP): gene and management
cards.back: APC tumor suppressor gene mutation. Thousands of colon polyps by teens; near 100% cancer risk. Prophylactic colectomy in young adulthood
Hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia — arteriovenous malformations cause recurrent GI bleeding and epistaxis
Gastroesophageal reflux disease. Treatment: lifestyle changes + PPI (most effective acid suppression)
Metaplastic change from squamous to columnar (intestinal) epithelium in distal esophagus due to chronic GERD. ↑ risk of esophageal adenocarcinoma
Squamous cell: upper 2/3, associated with alcohol/tobacco. Adenocarcinoma: lower 1/3, associated with GERD/Barrett's, obesity
Failure of lower esophageal sphincter to relax due to loss of inhibitory neurons (Auerbach plexus). Bird-beak sign on barium swallow, aperistalsis, dysphagia to both solids and liquids
Massive dilation of colon without mechanical obstruction. Seen post-surgery, in critically ill patients. Treatment: neostigmine (if Ccecum > 12 cm or > 3 days)
Children 6 months–3 years; intermittent colicky pain, currant jelly stool, 'sausage-shaped' mass in RUQ. Diagnostic and therapeutic: air/hydrostatic enema reduction
2% of population, 2 inches long, 2 feet from ileocecal valve, 2 types of ectopic tissue (gastric and pancreatic), presents by age 2, 2× more common in males
Elderly patient with sudden-onset left lower quadrant pain + rectal bleeding + bloody diarrhea. Watershed areas: splenic flexure, rectosigmoid junction
Acute: severe abdominal pain out of proportion to exam (SMA embolus). Chronic: postprandial pain (intestinal angina), food fear, weight loss
Diverticulosis: painless lower GI bleed (most common cause > 40 yr). Diverticulitis: left lower quadrant pain + fever + CT showing pericolonic fat stranding

cards.front: Carcinoid syndrome: features and tumor marker
cards.back: Carcinoid tumor with liver metastases (can't metabolize serotonin in liver). Flushing, diarrhea, bronchospasm, right heart valvular disease. ↑ 24-hr urinary 5-HIAA
Middle-aged women; pruritus, jaundice, fatigue. Anti-mitochondrial antibody (AMA), ↑ alkaline phosphatase. Granulomatous destruction of bile ducts

cards.front: Hepatitis C: treatment approach and cure definition
cards.back: Direct-acting antivirals (DAAs): NS5B polymerase inhibitor (sofosbuvir) + NS5A inhibitor (ledipasvir/velpatasvir) ± NS3/4A protease inhibitor (voxilaprevir). Preferred: sofosbuvir/velpatasvir (Epclusa) for all genotypes, or sofosbuvir/ledipasvir (Harvoni) for genotype 1. Duration: 8–12 weeks. Cure = SVR12 (sustained virologic response at 12 weeks). Test HCV RNA at weeks 4, 12, and 24 post-treatment.

cards.front: Acute liver failure: causes and management priorities
cards.back: Causes: acetaminophen overdose (most common in US), viral hepatitis, Wilson's disease, Budd-Chiari, AIH, ischemic hepatitis. Complications: hepatic encephalopathy (lactulose), coagulopathy (avoid FFP unless bleeding), cerebral edema (mannitol + ICP monitoring), hypoglycemia, infections (bacterial/fungal). Acetaminophen: N-acetylcysteine within 24 hours. Liver transplant evaluation if criteria met (King's College Criteria).
